stories.title: Devin Review and Quick Review in Windsurf
stories.summary: Windsurf has integrated two review modes — Devin Review and Quick Review — directly into the editor workspace. Devin Review appears to invoke a deeper, agent-driven verification pass (likely multi-step analysis similar to Devin's autonomous review pipeline), while Quick Review targets fast, lightweight feedback loops. Both eliminate the context switch between writing code and reviewing it, keeping the full workflow inside Windsurf. Developers can now trigger code verification without leaving the editor, which previously required bouncing between Windsurf and a separate review tool or browser.
stories.considerations: The most concrete win here is for solo developers or small teams doing self-review: instead of opening a PR, waiting for CI, and context-switching to GitHub's review UI, you get verification feedback inline while the code is still hot in your working memory — measurably tightening the write-verify loop. The non-obvious implication is that embedding review inside the authoring environment risks normalizing skipping async peer review entirely, which matters for teams where review is also a knowledge-transfer mechanism, not just a bug-catch. This lands at a moment when AI-assisted coding is generating code faster than review processes can absorb it — the bottleneck has shifted from writing to verifying, and in-editor review directly attacks that. The honest gotcha: the excerpt gives zero specifics on what 'Devin Review' actually checks — static analysis, semantic correctness, security, style — so until the actual capability surface is documented, it's hard to know whether this replaces a linter, a human reviewer, or something in between.

stories.title: New PR review experience with parallel execution
stories.summary: Cursor 0.43 (labeled 'Cursor 3') ships two distinct changes: a redesigned PR review UI and parallel plan execution via async subagents. The PR interface adds inline review threads, a commit history view, and file-tree navigation — consolidating the full PR lifecycle (create, review, merge) inside the editor. The parallel execution feature introduces a 'Build in Parallel' button that analyzes a plan's dependency graph, dispatches independent tasks to concurrent subagents, and serializes only steps with explicit dependencies. No published benchmark numbers on speedup, but the mechanism is genuine parallelism via async subagents rather than interleaved scheduling. Users can now run multi-step agentic plans without waiting for unrelated tasks to finish sequentially, and review PRs without leaving Cursor for GitHub's web UI.
stories.considerations: The parallel subagent model has a concrete payoff on plans like 'add auth middleware, write tests, update three independent service files' — tasks with no shared state can fan out simultaneously, cutting wall-clock time roughly proportional to the number of independent branches. The non-obvious risk: subagents operating in parallel on the same repo can produce write conflicts or divergent context if the dependency graph is misclassified — a task Cursor's planner marks as independent may actually share a config file or type definition, leading to silent merge collisions that only surface at the serialization step. This lands at a moment when the main complaint about agentic coding tools is latency on multi-file tasks; sequential execution on a 20-step plan is a real productivity ceiling, and this directly attacks it. The honest gotcha: 'Build in Parallel' appears to be a manual trigger, not automatic — developers need to recognize when to invoke it, and the dependency analysis is only as good as Cursor's plan parser, so complex implicit dependencies (shared global state, generated files) may not be caught before parallel agents diverge.

stories.title: gpt-realtime-2 model release
stories.summary: OpenAI released gpt-realtime-2, a new model available across three endpoints: v1/realtime (bidirectional audio interaction), v1/realtime/translations (real-time spoken language translation), and v1/realtime/transcription_se (dedicated transcription, likely 'streaming edition'). The model upgrade implies improved audio understanding and generation quality over the original gpt-realtime, though OpenAI hasn't published specific latency benchmarks or context-window changes in the changelog entry. The dedicated translation endpoint is the most structurally significant addition — previously, translation required chaining transcription with a text model, adding latency and error surface. Developers can now build speech-to-speech translation pipelines and higher-quality voice agents without post-processing hacks.
stories.considerations: A concrete scenario: if you're building a multilingual customer support voice bot, the v1/realtime/translations endpoint collapses what was a three-step pipeline (transcribe → translate → synthesize) into a single WebSocket session, cutting round-trip latency by at least one full LLM inference hop. The non-obvious implication is that dedicated transcription and translation endpoints suggest OpenAI is disaggregating the realtime stack — meaning you can now use transcription or translation independently without spinning up a full bidirectional audio session, which has real cost implications for high-volume one-way audio workloads. This lands at a moment when voice AI is hitting production friction: developers have been vocal about the original realtime API's instability and model quality gaps versus Whisper-based pipelines, so a model bump with endpoint specialization directly addresses that. The gotcha: 'gpt-realtime-2' versioning with no published deprecation timeline for the original means you need to explicitly pin your endpoint calls now — silent model swaps in the realtime API have burned teams before when latency characteristics changed mid-deployment.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary: Continue.dev added workspace-directory filtering to session history, letting users scope the chat history panel to sessions from a specific project folder rather than viewing all sessions globally. The filter works by associating each session with the workspace root active at creation time, then surfacing a directory-based filter control in the history UI. No new API or config changes are documented — this is a UI/UX-layer change to the existing session persistence mechanism. Developers juggling multiple active projects can now pull up only the sessions relevant to the repo they're currently in, without manually scanning through unrelated chat history.
stories.considerations: If you're context-switching between, say, a frontend monorepo and a backend services repo throughout the day, this directly cuts the time spent hunting for a specific debugging session — previously you'd scroll through a flat list mixing both projects, now you filter to the relevant workspace root and see only those sessions. The non-obvious implication: session history is now implicitly tagged with workspace path at write time, which means sessions started before this release likely have no workspace metadata and will appear unfiltered or under an 'unknown' bucket — expect some historical noise. This addresses a real pain point as Continue usage scales: power users running 10+ sessions per day across multiple projects have been effectively losing institutional context in a flat history list. Worth noting that if you use Continue with a multi-root workspace (multiple folders open simultaneously in VS Code), it's unclear which root gets tagged — test this before relying on the filter in monorepo setups with multiple workspace roots.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Synchronous Middleware for Every Tool Call
stories.summary: Claude Code now ships a hooks system that lets you intercept every tool call in the agentic loop via synchronous middleware — shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts registered at 20+ named lifecycle points including SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolBatch, and Stop. Each hook receives full JSON context on stdin describing the pending tool call and its arguments; returning a non-zero exit code with a structured JSON body blocks execution and surfaces the rejection reason directly back to Claude as context for its next turn, meaning the model can adapt rather than silently fail. The mechanism is in-process and synchronous — not a webhook or async callback — so hooks run in the critical path before any filesystem write, shell command, or API call executes. You can now enforce org-wide invariants (no writes outside /src, no curl to external hosts, mandatory schema validation) that Claude itself cannot override, even in fully autonomous runs.
stories.considerations: Concrete scenario: you're running Claude Code against a 200-file monorepo with a strict database migration policy — every SQL file write must pass a linter before it lands. With a PreToolUse hook on the Write tool that shells out to sqlfluff, you catch malformed migrations before they hit the filesystem, and the rejection message ('column type mismatch on line 12') feeds back into Claude's context so it self-corrects rather than requiring a human interrupt. Non-obvious implication: because hooks run synchronously in the critical path, a slow HTTP endpoint or a hung LLM-prompt hook will stall the entire agentic loop — there's no timeout config mentioned in the docs, so a flaky hook is a full pipeline freeze, not a graceful degradation. This matters now because the main complaint with autonomous coding agents has been 'I can't trust it unsupervised' — hooks are the first mechanism that lets you define machine-enforceable guardrails rather than relying on prompt instructions Claude can reason its way around. Gotcha worth flagging: hooks are configured per-project in .claude/hooks.json, which means they live in the repo and can be modified by Claude itself during a session unless you explicitly add a hook that blocks writes to that path — a bootstrapping problem you need to solve on day one.
